skill.name: networkx
skill.description: Guides Python users through NetworkX graph creation, algorithms, data import and export, synthetic network generation, and visualization workflows.
skill.summary: Analyze, generate, import, export, and visualize graph data with NetworkX.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many issues, but representative review shows most are false positives from Markdown code fences and graph algorithm terminology. I found no executable skill scripts, prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration. Residual risk is low because the documentation includes local file/database write examples and visible external links.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences misclassified as shell execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are false positives. The flagged backticks delimit Python examples in documentation, and no skill hook executes shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines are Markdown fences and NetworkX Python examples. I found no subprocess, shell, or runtime execution wrapper in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Graph terminology misclassified as weak cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections are false positives. The flagged content discusses graph algorithms, hashable node identifiers, drawing colors, and layout algorithms, not cryptographic primitives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled lines contain graph-analysis terms and Python data model guidance. No hashing, encryption, signature, or password-storage implementation is present.

low_findings.title: Documentation includes local file and database write examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples that write GraphML, CSV, JSON, compressed graph files, and SQLite tables. This is legitimate NetworkX documentation, but users should review paths before running generated code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly write local files and a SQLite database, but the paths are static examples and there is no automatic execution or hidden write behavior.

low_findings.title: External documentation and product links are present
low_findings.description: The skill links to NetworkX resources and includes an optional suggestion for K-Dense Web. These are visible documentation links, not code-initiated network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain plain URLs and prose. I found no requests, fetch calls, telemetry, credential transmission, or automatic network behavior.

content.value_statement: Graph datasets are hard to model, inspect, and explain clearly. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical NetworkX workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to create undirected, directed, and multigraph structures in NetworkX.
Guides shortest paths, centrality, clustering, connectivity, PageRank, and community detection workflows.
Covers synthetic graph generators, including random, scale-free, small-world, lattice, and tree models.
Shows how to import and export graph data with GraphML, GML, JSON, CSV, pandas, NumPy, SciPy, and SQLite.
Provides visualization guidance for matplotlib, Plotly, PyVis, Graphviz, bipartite layouts, and 3D plots.
Highlights performance, reproducibility, and layout choices for larger graph analysis tasks.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, but it does not execute NetworkX code by itself.
Very large graphs may require specialized graph databases or distributed processing outside NetworkX.
Visualization examples depend on optional packages such as matplotlib, Plotly, PyVis, pydot, or Graphviz.
Generated code that reads or writes files should be reviewed before execution in sensitive directories.

use_cases.title: Explore Social Network Structure
use_cases.description: Build a graph from relationships, identify influential nodes, detect communities, and summarize structural patterns.

use_cases.title: Model Infrastructure Dependencies
use_cases.description: Represent services or routes as graphs, inspect connectivity, find shortest paths, and flag fragile components.

use_cases.title: Prepare Research Network Figures
use_cases.description: Choose layouts, scale nodes by metrics, label important entities, and export readable publication figures.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Graph
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a NetworkX graph from this list of relationships. Explain the graph type I should use and why.

prompt_templates.title: Measure Important Nodes
prompt_templates.prompt: Given my graph columns and goal, recommend centrality and clustering metrics. Explain what each metric means for my data.

prompt_templates.title: Import and Visualize Network Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a NetworkX workflow to load my edge data, clean attributes, compute communities, and create a readable visualization.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Large Graph Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this NetworkX analysis plan for a large sparse graph. Suggest faster algorithms, memory controls, and validation checks.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to analyze a transportation network with weighted routes.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a weighted graph, explains shortest path and connectivity checks, and lists expected route metrics.

output_examples.input: A researcher wants to visualize communities in a citation network.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes community detection, node sizing by citation influence, color groups, and a layout suited for dense clusters.

output_examples.input: An engineer needs to export graph metrics for a dashboard.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines metric selection, tabular export fields, reproducibility settings, and checks before writing output files.

best_practices: Choose the graph class that matches directionality and whether parallel edges matter.
Set random seeds for generated graphs and force-directed layouts when results must be reproducible.
Validate file paths, schema assumptions, and graph size before running import or export code.
anti_patterns: Do not use an undirected graph when edge direction changes the meaning of the analysis.
Do not compute expensive exact centrality metrics on very large graphs without checking scale first.
Do not save graph files or database tables to sensitive locations without reviewing the target path.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps design NetworkX workflows for graph creation, algorithms, input and output, generation, and visualization.

faq.question: Can it run NetworkX code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and example workflows. The user or host tool decides whether code is executed.

faq.question: Which graph types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Graph, DiGraph, MultiGraph, and MultiDiGraph, including when each type is appropriate.

faq.question: Does it support file import and export planning?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers common graph formats, pandas, NumPy, SciPy, JSON, CSV, GraphML, GML, and SQLite examples.

faq.question: Is it useful for visualization?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains layouts, labels, styling, interactive libraries, Graphviz export, bipartite plots, and 3D visualization.

faq.question: What should users review before running generated code?
faq.answer: Users should review file paths, database targets, graph size, package dependencies, and privacy requirements for the dataset.
